request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"wesabe::fintech","name":"Wesabe","one_liner":"Personal finance management website that analyzed consumer financial data and offered money-saving advice, shutting down in 2010 after losing to Mint.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both use a recurring subscription monetization model (Trusted facts confirm subscription_recurring for Wesabe). However, Wesabe targeted individual consumers with a single-tier subscription while the new pitch is B2B SaaS with a per-seat plus per-transaction overage pricing model aimed at mid-market corporate controllers — a materially different commercial structure."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Both operate in fintech and involve financial data processing. Beyond that, the markets diverge sharply: Wesabe served the US consumer personal finance management (PFM) market, whereas the new pitch targets US mid-market finance teams managing the monthly accounting close — a professional B2B workflow category with entirely different buyers, regulatory context, and willingness-to-pay."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Wesabe relied on direct consumer acquisition via its website and community features. The new pitch targets controllers at mid-market companies, implying a B2B sales motion (direct sales, finance-team champions, procurement cycles) — a fundamentally different go-to-market approach."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both are/were early-stage web platforms in the US fintech space seeking product-market fit with a relatively small team and external seed/Series A funding. The scale and operational complexity differ significantly given B2C vs. B2B customer bases, but the early-stage startup profile is broadly comparable."}},"why_similar":"Both are US-based fintech web platforms in the financial data automation space using a recurring subscription monetization model. Both face the challenge of winning user trust with sensitive financial data and must differentiate against well-funded incumbents (Mint for Wesabe; legacy ERP/close-management tools for the new pitch). The early-stage, venture-backed profile and US geography are also shared.","where_diverged":"The new pitch diverges from Wesabe on at least four concrete dimensions: (1) Customer type — B2B mid-market controllers vs. individual consumers; (2) Business model — per-seat SaaS with transaction-based overage tiers vs. a flat consumer subscription; (3) Go-to-market — enterprise/mid-market sales cycles and controller champions vs. direct-to-consumer web acquisition; (4) Sub-sector — automated accounting close workflow vs. personal finance management/budgeting advice.","failure_causes":["lost product-market fit to superior competitor (Mint)","inferior user experience relative to Mint's automated bank-sync","over-reliance on manual data entry creating friction","inability to differentiate on feature depth or community","failure to convert free/trial users into paying subscribers at scale","single product in a winner-take-most consumer PFM market"],"lessons_for_input":["Build a defensible moat through deep workflow integration (e.g., ERP connectors, audit trails) before a better-funded competitor can replicate your core feature set.","Prioritize UX and automation from day one — mid-market controllers will abandon a tool that requires manual data entry just as consumer users abandoned Wesabe for Mint's auto-sync.","Target a segment where switching costs are structurally high (accounting close has compliance and audit dependencies) to avoid the commodity trap that killed Wesabe.","Validate willingness to pay and per-seat overage pricing with real controller buyers early; Wesabe never clearly established a monetization wedge against its free-tier competitor.","Avoid winner-take-most dynamics by owning a niche (e.g., specific ERP integrations or industries) rather than competing head-on with broad incumbents on day one."]}
